paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act must absolutely include videogames. They are after all digital products that we purchase and own, or in many case licenses with an indefinite duration, that they can just end whenever they want. Much like a customer buys a physical product or a service, we are customers of digital goods and services as much as physical goods and services nowadays. A lot of big companies that publish videogames act in an anti-consumer manner, revoking access whenever they want bigger profit margins, with no warning whatsoever. They also, mostly outside of the EU, fire thousands of their employees without much warning at all, to increase their profit margins. While this isn't about the employees, although it's a huge positive that there are laws for fair treatment for employees, that's just how a lot of those companies operate. There's also laws for customer rights when it comes to physical products in the EU and videogames nowadays are a huge percentage of digital sales. Even if the estimations of the gaming industry being 'bigger' than the movie interesting were inaccurate, it's still a massive market, in which huge publishing companies like Microsoft, EA, Ubisoft are largely uncontrolled. They are the main reason for the "Stop Destroying Videogames" initiative that has reached near 1.5 million signatures. The only real difference to buying a digital product such as a videogame, compared to buying a physical product is that one is digital and the other is not. Our money still goes to a company that provides a product/service, which, very importantly, is often advertised as something it isn't. Just as it empowers companies with huge profit margins to fire people whenever it suits them, it empowers them all the same to sell something that they can take away whenever they like, without any warning whatsoever. There's almost zero videogames that notify you of that happening. It used to be that you buy a videogame and it's yours, because you own it, but that's not true anymore quite a lot of the time. If these companies can just decide they can do whatever they want with our money, we must have a law that protects us against that. Just like a company that sells you a physical product has to keep their advertised promises, for instance a few years warranty, a company that sells you a digital good as expensive as a videogame can be, absolutely has to keep their advertised promises. Videogames nowadays can cost up to 80 euros as a basic price and then special editions can go in the hundreds easily. They aren't cheap at all.
